channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Russia Strikes Ukraine as Explosions Rock Capital of Kyiv
item.description: At least 13 people were killed in the assault, which began hours after President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine warned that Russia was preparing another “massive strike” on Kyiv.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd
item.media:description: Rescuers on Thursday at an apartment building in Kyiv that was damaged in overnight Russian strikes.

item.title: Crypto Brought Trump a Huge Windfall, Even as Many Investors Lost Big
item.description: President Trump and his family reaped vast financial rewards from a memecoin that generated losses for hundreds of thousands of investors.
item.dc:creator: Eric Lipton, Andrea Fuller and David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump amassed enormous wealth during the first year of his second term, with $636 million of it coming from his cryptocurrency.

item.title: Does Trump Worry About Conflicts of Interest? ‘I Found Out That Nobody Cared.’
item.description: American presidents have generally tried to avoid appearing to profit from the office. President Trump has chosen a different path.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.media:description: President Trump, followed by his sons Donald Jr. and Eric, steps off the new Air Force One in Bismarck, N.D., on Wednesday.

item.title: The Key Ways Trump’s Financial Interests Intersect With Government Policy
item.description: President Trump’s business holdings, which garnered him more than $2 billion last year, create potential conflicts of interest surpassing any predecessor.
item.dc:creator: Ben Protess, Andrea Fuller and Eric Lipton
item.media:description: President Trump’s financial investments have vastly escalated during this second term.

item.title: Promising Much to Many, Johnson Loses His Grip on the House
item.description: Republicans are growing frustrated with Mr. Johnson’s approach to governing with a razor-thin majority, saying that he promises more than he can achieve, frustrating the disparate groups in his caucus.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold and Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Demetrius Freeman for The New York Times

item.title: Colorado Governor Fires Officials Who Opposed Freeing Tina Peters
item.description: Gov. Jared Polis commuted the sentence of Tina Peters, who tampered with voting machines in an attempt to show that the 2020 election had been rigged against President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy
item.media:description: Gov. Jared Polis, a Democrat under pressure from the president, asked the clemency board to reconsider the case.

item.title: The Democratic Incumbents Most at Risk of Losing to Progressive Primary Challengers in 2026
item.description: A wave of outsider fervor has already knocked out a number of incumbents in primary races. Several more face spirited opposition.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:description: Melat Kiros, a 29-year-old democratic socialist, defeated Representative Diana DeGette of Colorado, a 68-year-old incumbent running for a 16th term on Tuesday.

item.title: Judge Blocks Postal Service From Imposing Restrictions on Mail-In Ballots
item.description: The ruling, based on agreements the service made in a 2021 legal settlement, was the second time a judge has blocked new curbs by President Trump on voting by mail.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague and Adam Sella
item.category: National Assn for the Advancement of Colored People
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mail-in ballots being sorted in City of Industry, Calif., last year.

item.title: Trump Remakes Washington, D.C., Into a Maze of Fences and Guard Members
item.description: President Trump seems to have turned swaths of the city into either a construction zone or an armed camp as he seeks to prove that he alone can improve it.
item.dc:creator: Katie Rogers
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: Federal officials have effectively closed off access to the Reflecting Pool ahead of the nation’s 250th anniversary.

item.title: America, Onscreen
item.description: Ten writers on the films that get at the hopes, dreams, heartbreak and hilarity of the U.S.A. as it turns 250.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: What America’s 250th Means to Mamdani
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani has not actively pushed New York City’s role in the nation’s anniversary celebrations, in part because of the complexities he sees in the country’s past and present.
item.dc:creator: Emma Goldberg
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani has often seemed most comfortable expressing a sort of New York City-focused patriotism, rather than one rooted in the nation’s history.

item.title: Trump Finds His Presidential Alter Ego, the ‘He-Man’ Teddy Roosevelt
item.description: President Trump flew into town on the new Air Force One and spent time touring the library dedicated to Theodore Roosevelt, whom he called “a great he-man.”
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.media:description: President Trump arrived on a train at the Theodore Roosevelt Presidential Library in North Dakota on Wednesday.

item.title: Immigrant Arrests Surge to 10,000 in 5 Days as ICE Clamps Down
item.description: The agency has doubled its daily arrest numbers without the fanfare of last year’s large urban operations, sowing fear in immigrant communities.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:description: Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents patrolling a federal court building in New York last month.

item.title: Trump Administration Delivers Lucrative Win for Its Kratom Allies
item.description: In moving to ban a potent synthetic version of kratom, the president’s team paved the way for more sales for makers of rival botanic supplements, who had aggressively lobbied for the change.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett and Kenneth P. Vogel
item.media:credit: Nick Oxford for The New York Times
item.media:description: After President Trump’s victory, companies selling kratom supplements and their executives ramped up their spending on lobbyists with ties to his administration and donations to his political operation.

item.title: Bureau of Prisons Will Close Facilities Housing Thousands of Inmates
item.description: The agency pointed to crumbling infrastructure, chronic staffing shortages and budget shortfalls as it moved to cut costs.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.media:credit: Julia Robinson for The New York Times
item.media:description: The federal prison in Big Spring, Texas, is among the facilities to close.

item.title: N.Y.P.D. Memo Outlines Taylor Swift Wedding Events at the Garden
item.description: Law enforcement officials in the city are preparing to monitor the pop star’s two-day event at Madison Square Garden, and several streets in the area will be closed.
item.dc:creator: Maria Cramer, Chelsia Rose Marcius and Emma G. Fitzsimmons
item.media:description: Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce’s plans are expected to require hundreds of police officers.

item.title: What Would Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce’s Prenuptial Agreement Look Like?
item.description: What would a prenuptial agreement between one of the world’s biggest pop stars and a three-time Super Bowl champion look like? Legal experts weigh in.
item.dc:creator: Aruni Soni
item.media:description: Taylor Swift has assets worth nearly $2 billion. Travis Kelce is estimated to have between $70 million and $90 million.

item.title: U.S. Resumes Dollar Transfers to Iraq After Monthslong Suspension
item.description: The Trump administration had halted the shipments to Iraq as part of its efforts to pressure the Baghdad government to distance itself from Iran.
item.dc:creator: Raja Abdulrahim and Falih Hassan
item.media:description: Iraq’s prime minister, Ali al-Zaidi, has taken steps to try to rein in Iran-backed militias in his country.

item.title: JD Vance Defends Iran War in Speech to Military Members
item.description: The vice president, a staunch critic of foreign wars, accused the president’s detractors of trying to prolong a conflict the administration was trying to end.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Before becoming the vice president, JD Vance, a former Marine, was a fierce critic of the United States’ “forever wars.”

item.title: Ruling on Trans Athletes Gave the G.O.P. a Win. Most Democrats Looked the Other Way.
item.description: While Republicans celebrated the ruling, many Democrats stayed quiet on an issue that had proved divisive in the last election.
item.dc:creator: Emily Davies and Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Republicans celebrated the Supreme Court’s ruling this week upholding laws banning transgender athletes from competing in women’s sports. Democrats were largely silent on the matter.

item.title: Nine Arrested in Federal Crackdown on L.A.’s Sex-Trafficking Corridor
item.description: Federal officials in Los Angeles described gang members’ yearslong scheme that relied on social media and physical violence to recruit and maintain control of the girls.
item.dc:creator: Emily Baumgaertner Nunn

item.title: Can Companies Embrace A.I. Without Layoffs? This One Says It Is Trying to.
item.description: The German software giant SAP says it is betting that employees can reinvent jobs instead of eliminating them. Experts are divided on whether it will work.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.media:credit: Lena Mucha for The New York Times
item.media:description: Matthias Deindl, a product management executive for the company SAP, at an A.I.-enhanced production line in Walldorf, Germany.

item.title: How Trump Made ‘Y.M.C.A.’ His Anthem, Despite the Village People and Victor Willis’s Mixed Feelings
item.description: On social media, he said the Village People’s lead singer, Victor Willis, was with him “right from the beginning.” But the president has a more complicated history with the group.
item.dc:creator: Aishvarya Kavi
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: President-elect Donald J. Trump danced to “Y.M.C.A.” along with the Village People during a pre-inauguration event in January last year.

item.title: Victor Willis, Lead Singer of the Village People, Dies at 74
item.description: Often performing dressed as a helmeted police officer, he also co-wrote the group’s biggest hits, including “Y.M.C.A.” and “Macho Man.”
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall and Isabella Kwai
item.media:description: Victor Willis performing with the Village People in Chicago in 1979. More recently, he became a polarizing figure because of his (and the group’s music’s) association with President Trump.

item.title: Climbers Get Engaged Atop Empire State Building. Then They Get Arrested.
item.description: After two extreme climbers ascended the landmark’s 200-foot needle, one proposed to the other, before they were apprehended by the police.
item.dc:creator: Andy Newman, Maia Coleman and Chelsia Rose Marcius

item.title: The World Has Failed the Uyghurs
item.description: If the global community can’t stop China’s atrocities against the Uyghurs, it must help us keep our culture alive in exile.
item.dc:creator: Tahir Imin
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity

item.title: U.S.A.I.D. Cuts Killed People. That’s the Truth.
item.description: Musk insists that his demolition of humanitarian aid didn’t cost lives. So I propose he take a trip with me.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kristof
item.category: President's Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:credit: Saidu Bah for The New York Times
item.media:description: Moriba Freeman’s pregnant daughter, Yamah Freeman, in yellow, bled to death en route to a hospital.
